coredata.dc:title: Application of a primer passivated CdTe quantum dots as a novel Förster resonance energy transfer based nanobiosensor for Covid-19 virus detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bardajee, Ghasem Rezanejade
Sharifi, Mahdieh
Khanahmadi, Masoumeh
coredata.dc:description: This study highlighted the significance of quantum dots in nanobiosensors for detecting viral infections, noting their high sensitivity and specificity. A novel primer-based nanobiosensor utilizing the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism with quantum dots has been developed for the detection of the Coronavirus disease (2019). This system employed cadmium telluride semiconducting nanocrystal quantum dots modified with a specific thiol-labeled primer serving as a fluorescent label. A complementary target deoxyribonucleic acid, designed to match a specific region of the Coronavirus disease (2019) genome, formed a sandwich hybrid on the quantum dots’ surface. The primers selected for detection were tailored to the most conserved and prevalent strains of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, ensuring both accuracy and specificity in testing. This biosensor could analyze nasal-pharyngeal swab samples through straightforward procedures, yielding rapid results. The limit of detection for this quantum dot based biosensor was 1.71 × 10⁻⁹ mol/L. Tests on actual patient samples demonstrated that this Förster resonance energy transfer -based QD biosensor aligned well with real-time polymerase chain reaction results. Unlike traditional polymerase chain reaction methods used in virus processing facilities, this optical technique enabled quick and easy microorganism detection, facilitating crucial decision-making for maintaining product quality and safety under 15 min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Förster resonance energy transfer, Nanobiosensors
Coronavirus disease (2019)
Cadmium telluride
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of QDs based biosensor for detecting ctDNA
Depiction of the primer modified CdTe quantum dots (pQDs)
Conjugation of thiolated DNA to QDs using ligand exchange process
Performance of nanobiosensor for detecting complementary target DNA (ctDNA)
Limitations of the detection of designed nanobiosensor

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic view of the novel diagnostic strategy for detection of SARS-CoV-2.

(A) DLS of CdTe QDs, and (B) DLS, (C) SEM, (D) TEM, (E) AFM images (F), and histogram of AFM images of primer modified CdTe QDs. (G) UV spectra of Cy3, CdTe QDs and pQDs with normalized fluorescence of CdTe QDs.

(A) The effect of thiolated primer/QD ratio on fluorescence performance of QDs. (B) Influence of pH, and (C) influence of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of thiolated primer/QD conjugate at a ratio of 1:1 (λex for emission spectra = 325 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the nanobiosensor were analyzed with varying micromolar concentrations of ctDNA (ranging from 0 to 0.023 µM) in RNase-free water. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra were also examined for the nanobiosensor with different micromolar concentrations of ctDNA in Tris buffer (0–0.023 µM), using an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. (C) The hybrid system exhibited distinct performance characteristics in differentiating between the target DNA, three-base mismatched DNA, and non-complementary DNA molecules in RNase-free water.
A schematic representation for the formation of a hybrid system, and a non-hybride system.

(A) Calibration plot for pQDs nanobiosensor in the residence of distinct concentrations of ctDNA based on the Stern-Volmer equation. (B) Fluorescence spectra of some real samples including SARS-CoV-2 (V1 and V2 (positive)) or without that (V3 (negative)).
Summary of advantages and limitations of COVID-19 diagnostic method.

row.entry: Requires trained personnel and lab equipment, time-consuming, reagent shortages
Dependent on primer design and specificity
Generally lower sensitivity than nucleic acid mplification tests (NAATs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A typical recipe for FL measurements of QDs biosensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Total volume was 170 μL that X = 3 μL of quencher DNA was added to the sample.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Y = 0–7 μL complementary or noncomplementary or RNA from saliva samples from suspected COVID-19 patients.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Details of real sample test for fifty human throat swab samples using pQDs nanobiosensor.

head.ce:title: Application of a primer passivated CdTe quantum dots as a novel Förster resonance energy transfer based nanobiosensor for Covid-19 virus detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran 19839-63113, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, 19839-63113, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academic Center for Education, Culture & Research (ACECR), Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academic Center for Education, Culture & Research (ACECR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academic Center for Education, Culture & Research (ACECR), Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study highlighted the significance of quantum dots in nanobiosensors for detecting viral infections, noting their high sensitivity and specificity. A novel primer-based nanobiosensor utilizing the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism with quantum dots has been developed for the detection of the Coronavirus disease (2019). This system employed cadmium telluride semiconducting nanocrystal quantum dots modified with a specific thiol-labeled primer serving as a fluorescent label. A complementary target deoxyribonucleic acid, designed to match a specific region of the Coronavirus disease (2019) genome, formed a sandwich hybrid on the quantum dots’ surface. The primers selected for detection were tailored to the most conserved and prevalent strains of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2, ensuring both accuracy and specificity in testing. This biosensor could analyze nasal-pharyngeal swab samples through straightforward procedures, yielding rapid results. The limit of detection for this quantum dot based biosensor was 1.71 × 10⁻⁹ mol/L. Tests on actual patient samples demonstrated that this Förster resonance energy transfer -based QD biosensor aligned well with real-time polymerase chain reaction results. Unlike traditional polymerase chain reaction methods used in virus processing facilities, this optical technique enabled quick and easy microorganism detection, facilitating crucial decision-making for maintaining product quality and safety under 15 min.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Coronavirus disease (2019) (COVID-19) pandemic, effected by the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2), has led to an unprecedented number of contagion, hospitalizations, and deaths worldwide. Despite significant attempt to control the pandemic—such as developing vaccines and striving for herd immunity—the post-acute phase remains a major concern [1]. On one hand, mutations SARS-CoV-2 were emerging rapidly, particularly changes in the spike (S) protein happened quickly that either enhanced transmissibility or enabled immune evasion, presenting significant challenges to pandemic control and treatment. However, the way the virus managed to acquire so many advantageous mutations in such a short time period remains unclear [2]. On the other hand, some side effects were reported following the introduction of SARS-CoV-2 vaccine. Headaches were the most commonly reported, affecting 30 %-51 % of individuals. Other side effects included cerebrovascular diseases, venous sinus thrombosis, ischemic stroke, intracerebral bleeding, and more. Additionally, recent reports from countries like Brazil and Canada have noted some cases of SARS-CoV-2 infection in domestic and street animals, as well as among homeless populations. Anyway, in such diseases, quick and early detection of the virus is very effective. As a result, our findings highlighted the importance and value of integrating various data sources for effective research and public health monitoring using advanced primer-based biosensors [3].

These biosensors can utilize nucleic acid primers labeled with quantum dots (QDs) to accurately detect viral Ribonucleic acid (RNA) or Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) through specific target sequences. These biosensors have the potential to significantly enhance viral infection diagnosis and management since they frequently provide high sensitivity, specificity, and quick response times. Functional nucleic acids (FNAs) are RNA or DNA molecules that have the ability to either aid in chemical reactions (DNAzymes and ribozymes) or bind specific targets (DNA and RNA aptamers). These compounds can be extracted from sizable DNA or RNA libraries by in vitro selection employing a particular target of interest as the binding or activation partner, or by systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX). Compared to traditional protein-based systems, FNA-based biosensors have a number of benefits, including cost-effectiveness, scalability in manufacturing, and precise probe engineering for utilization in complex host environments. These biosensors have proven to be effective in identifying a variety of viruses, including those that impact food safety, livestock, agriculture, and human health [4–7]. Monitoring and quantifying the signal aids in determining viral load, facilitating pathogen identification and disease progression [8]. QDs based biosensors exhibit high sensitivity and specificity in detecting a wide range of virus infections, including respiratory viruses, Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), hepatitis B and C, and herpes simplex virus (HSV), which can cause severe or fatal diseases. Furthermore, these studies open up new avenues for various early detection methodologies, such as biosensors, a low-cost, highly reliable device that can quickly detect the disease even in asymptomatic conditions [6,9,10]. These biosensors are easy to use and cost-effective, and they offer rapid results, making them valuable in clinical, diagnostic, and research settings. Their potential as a detection strategy for challenging viral infections is promising. In simple terms, the latest advancements in biosensor technologies could make it possible for diagnostic tests to be conducted at the point of care (for example, in a doctor’s office or at home) with results that are as good as or even better than the standards currently accepted for cost, time, and accuracy [11,12], contributing to improved health outcomes and reduced global burden of viral diseases, and playing a significant role in the disease’s subsequent decline [13–15]. However, various strategies exist for diagnosing COVID-19, primarily focusing on viral nucleic acid detection, antigen detection, and the assessment of the host’s immune responses [16–18]. Each diagnostic method has its own strengths and weaknesses as listed in Table 1.

The newly developed point of care diagnostic method utilizes the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and gold nanorods (Au-NRs) to detect the COVID-19 virus quickly and conveniently. This innovation offers a promising approach for rapid testing and controlling the spread of COVID-19. Additionally, this design has the potential to be adapted for detecting other related antigens, antibodies, and proteins [19].

As a result, to expand the role of FRET method, these studies have focused on utilizing quantum dots biosensors for the quick detection of SARS-CoV-2, which is the virus responsible for COVID-19. Quantum dots offer many benefits compared to customary carbon dots. They are smaller, and more efficient. They can also be used in a variety of applications spanning multiple fields. This makes them a preferred choice for modern technology. QDs have outstanding light absorption and efficient charge separation, so they are great for photovoltaics and light-emitting devices. In many sensing applications, they effectively improve their performance by being much more selective, and sensitive than carbon dots (CDs), which importantly improve both their detectability, and efficiency. Higher short-circuit current densities can be provided by QDs, which lead to important improvements in energy conversion efficiency that are greatly helpful [20,21]. In this regard, researchers developed a novel primer probe based on surface-modified cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs) using a ligand exchange process. These primer-based sensors make use of the FRET mechanism for detection, and enabling the identification of the virus. To ensure the effectiveness of these biosensors, the researchers conducted an optimization of preparation conditions for the QDs biosensors, ensuring that the sensors are well-prepared for accurate and efficient detection. Additionally, they carried out a thorough system characterization, which likely involved understanding the biosensor’s behavior under different conditions, such as varying concentrations of the virus or environmental factors. Moreover, the study’s significance is underscored by the evaluation of field sample testing for real-time rapid detection of the COVID-19 causative virus (SARS-CoV-2) [19,22,23]. This down to earth testing is pivotal and illustrates the potential for real-world application, appearing the guarantee for improving infection determination and observation endeavors related to COVID-19. In general, these inquiries present a critical progression in biosensor innovation, possibly advertising a profitable instrument for the convenient and exact discovery of SARS-CoV-2, which seem to have a noteworthy effect on infection reconnaissance and conclusion within the setting of the continuous COVID-19 widespread [24].

In the current research, probes for SARS-CoV-2 virus detection were designed using quantum dots modified with specific primers, targeting distinct regions of the virus. Their performance was evaluated with two key principles in mind:

A) The probes were designed based on specific viral regions commonly used in RT-PCR assays, and B) The distance between the dye and the quencher in the final hybrid system was adjusted. In some configurations, the dye was placed at the start of the chain and the quencher was placed at the end on the complementary DNA, while in others, the dye and quencher were positioned side by side in the middle of the chain. This allowed the FRET efficiency in different system designs to be assessed. In this study, surface-modified CdTe quantum dots (QDs) equipped with capture DNA, and designed based on a specific fragment of the SARS-CoV-2 genome, were utilized to identify target complementary DNA or RNA from the SARS-CoV-2 virus through a FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer) experiment. In this system, quantum dots are placed as donors in the FRET system at the beginning of the chain (in 5′ part), and the Cy3 dye as a quencher in the FRET system is positioned in the middle of the chain, allowing the performance of the system with this specific sequence to be evaluated. The CdTe QDs, capped with thioglycolic acid (TGA), were synthesized precisely in an aqueous solution. The surface-bound TGA molecules were replaced by thiolated DNA oligonucleotides (capture DNA) through a ligand exchange process, resulting in DNA-conjugated QDs (QDs-DNA). These DNA-conjugated QDs exhibit strong photoluminescence (PL) and excellent stability. As illustrated in Fig. 1, they serve as fluorescent nanoprobes for detecting target DNA sequences (complementary DNA (ctDNA)) or viral RNA, using the classic “sandwich” structure in combination with the FRET experiment. In the absence of target DNA (non-complementary DNA), there was no interaction between the quencher DNA (Cy3-labeled DNA) and the QDs-DNA. However, when both target DNA (complementary DNA) and quencher DNA (Cy3-labeled DNA) were present, a sandwich complex formed. This hybridization resulted in a reduction of QD-DNA emission intensity due to energy transfer from the CdTe QDs (donor) to the Cy3 organic quencher (acceptor). The novelty of this research lies in the development of a novel nanobiosensor based on FRET with quantum dots for detecting SARS-CoV-2. This is a departure from traditional detection techniques like PCR. The use of CdTe quantum dots functionalized with thiol-labeled primers as a fluorescent label is a pioneering approach. The design of the primers tailored to conserved regions of SARS-CoV-2 ensures accuracy and cross-strain specificity, addressing a critical need in viral detection. Demonstrating alignment with real-time PCR results using patient samples adds significant clinical relevance, establishing the biosensor's reliability in real-world applications. This rapid detection capability is highly beneficial for urgent decision-making in healthcare and public health

Thioglycolic acid (TGA, 98 %, molecular weight (MW): 92.11 g/mol), CdCl2. 5 H2O (99.99 %, MW: 237.50 g/mol), and tellurium (Te) powder (99 %, MW: 127.60 g/mol) were purchased from Sigma. All other analytical grade chemicals were supplied by Merck Chemical Co. DNA oligonucleotides (100 μmol L−1), purified by high performance liquid chromatography, were obtained from Metabion, Germany, and their sequences were as follows:
Thiol-labeled probe (thiolated IP4): 5′-Thiol-C6-CCTGGCGTG-3′, MW: 3059 Da
Quencher DNA (Cy3-labeled IP4): 5′-Cy3- GTTTGTATGA −3′, MW: 3581 Da
Complementary target DNA (ctDNA): 5′- TCATACAAACCACGCCAGG −3′ (MW: 5199 Da): This mapped to a SARS-CoV-2 section genome (https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov.
Non-complementary target DNA (non-ctDNA): 5′- GTGACATGACATCCGTTCGTGA −3′, MW: 6465 Da
Three-bases-mismatched DNA: 5′- TCATACAAACCACGCCTCC −3′, MW: 4864 Da.

A range of advanced techniques were employed to characterize and evaluate the performance of the quantum dot (QD)-based biosensor. The Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrometer was utilized to analyze the optical properties of the QDs, and it was prepared with user-friendly computer program that encouraged information procurement and examination. To check the QDs at the nanoscale, a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) from Philips CM 10-H T was utilized. This instrument works at 300 kV and is competent of high-resolution imaging, permitting the examination of the QDs' structure and morphology. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was conducted using a Horiba SZ-100 system, which provides data on the size distribution and stability of the quantum dots (QDs) in solution. For scanning electron microscopy (SEM), a FEI Quanta 200 magnifying instrument was employed. SEM gives high-resolution pictures of the QDs' surface morphology and permits for the examination of their intelligence with other materials, which is pivotal for understanding the biosensor's usefulness. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was conducted using a Computerized Disobedient Nanowizards system from Germany. AFM operates in a tapping mode (TM), which involves the use of a cantilever with a sharp tip that taps on the surface of the QDs. This mode is particularly suitable for studying the topography and mechanical properties of the QDs without causing significant damages.
Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were gathered at room temperature using a Shimadzu UV–vis spectrophotometer from Japan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of QDs based biosensor for detecting ctDNA
ce:section.ce:para:
Following the synthesis of CdTe-TGA QDs [25], a NaHTe solution was prepared by dissolving 0.025 g of Te powder (0.196 mmol), and 0.025 g (0.661 mmol) of NaBH4 in 2.5 mL of nitrogen-bubbled double-distilled water, stirred for 120 minutes. The following lines have described more details about the chemical reaction that occurred in this step:
(1) 4NaBH4 + 2Te + 7H2O → 2NaHTe + Na2B4O7 + 14H2↑

Simultaneously, a Cd2+ precursor solution was made by dissolving 0.1 g (0.546 mmol) of CdCl2 in 80 mL of nitrogen-bubbled double-distilled water, to which TGA (1.4 mmol, 0.2 mL) was added. The pH was adjusted to 8–9 using 1 M NaOH, and the NaHTe solution was introduced, resulting in a color change from colorless to yellow. The mixture was then heated to 100 °C for one hour under nitrogen, with 100 µL samples taken every 10 min. The reaction was quenched by cooling the mixture to 0 °C in an ice bath. In the final step, the purification of CdTe-TGA quantum dots was carried out using spin filters (Ultra-4, MWCO 5 kDa), spinning at 9000 rpm for 15 minutes.

The next step involved the conjugation of thiolated capture DNA with CdTe QDs using the ligand exchange process for fabricating QDs-DNA probe. For this case, purified CdTe-TGA QDs (100 µL) were mixed with a solution of thiolated DNA (5′-thiol -C6-CCTGGCGTG-3′, 100 µL, 0.6 µM) and stirred at room temperature. The thiol groups of the DNA were expected to interact with the metals on the QD surface, forming QDs-DNA. The optical properties of the solution were monitored via fluorescence (FL) measurements at different time points during the ligand-exchange process. Finally, the solution was collected for further experimentation.

Next, according to Table 2, ctDNA detection was performed by initially mixing 150 μL of solvent (either RNase-free water or buffer) with the modified quantum dots (QDs-DNA). This was followed by the addition of 3 μL of quencher DNA (0.06 μM) and then 7 μL of ctDNA (0.06 μM) to reach the final volume, all at room temperature (Table 2). Subsequently, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded over a range of 460–630 nm, using an excitation wavelength of 325 nm.

Saliva samples from suspected COVID-19 patients were collected by the National Institute for Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology in Iran, and the RNA was extracted. The extracted RNA was then added to a mixture of modified QDs and quencher DNA (5′-Cy3-GTTTGTATGA-3′), resulting in a total volume of 170 μL. The presence of COVID-19 was detected using the nanobiosensor through a fluorescence monitoring system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Depiction of the primer modified CdTe quantum dots (pQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The widespread COVID-19 pandemic has led to a universal health crisis, highlighting the urgent need for accurate and efficient diagnostic tests. This study aimed to develop a novel method for detecting the presence of SARS-CoV-2 in patient saliva samples using a quantum dots based nanobiosensor. At first, nanobiosensor was identified as the main component of the system before and after modification by the desired primer oligonucleotide chain. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was employed to conduct particle size analysis. This technique allows the assessment of particle size and aggregation behavior, which are critical for ensuring the effectiveness of the QDs-based biosensor. By analyzing the scattering patterns of light, researchers could determine the hydrodynamic radius of the QDs, offering insights into their dispersion and stability in various environments. This strategy is especially valuable for understanding the behavior of the QDs at the nanoscale and the way it may affect their execution within the biosensor [26,27]. Measurements of the samples were performed at a temperature of 25 ± 1 °C. Deionized water was utilized to dilute the suspensions prior to analysis. The particle size data has been shown in Fig. 2A and B. The particle sizes of QDs and pQDs (QDs-DNA) were between 1.95 nm and 12 nm, respectively, with a dispersibility < 0.200. SEM images demonstrated the uniform dispersion and smooth surface morphology of pQDs (Fig. 2C). Proper sample preparation is essential for TEM analysis of any specimen. Average particle sizes of bare CdTe QDs in aqueous solutions were measured by TEM (Fig. 2 D). The sizes obtained with this method was 6.6 nm. Additionally, the HRTEM (Fig. 2E) images of CdTe QDs revealed that the interplanar spacings (lattice fringe) were approximately 3.27 nm, corresponding to the (200) plane of the cubic CdTe crystallite. Samples for examining pQDs were arranged by drying each pQDs solution on a lacey carbon Cu (300 mesh) grid that had been previously coated with a graphene monolayer (thickness = 0.3 nm) to reduce the impact of the supporting film. All imaging analyses were conducted using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) equipped with a probe Cs corrector. TEM image revealed the nano size of pQDs with mean diameters of 11 nm. Spherical structures like nanoparticles were observed in the TEM analysis (Fig. 2F). The AFM images clarified the structural in the thin films as presented in Fig. 2G and H. The structural can be realized by different parameters which quantify the root mean square (RMS) roughness of a surface. The RMS roughness value can be determined from the cross-sectional profile or a surface area. [28]. The RMS roughness found for pQDs thin film was 2.9 nm. UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cy3 and CdTe QDs with normalized fluorescence of CdTe QDs have been shown in Fig. 2G suggesting potential optical application in FRET effect. The absorption peaks at 490 nm and 510 nm were related to QDs and pQDs, respectively. Besides, the absorption peak at 550 nm was for Cy3. When the excitation wavelength was 375 nm, double emission spectrum could be obtained. The full width half maximum (fwhm) was approximately 40 nm for QDs. The strong spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of the quencher DNA (Cy3-labeled IP4) indicated the successful operation of the FRET mechanism in the detection of desired virus (Fig. 2I) acting as the acceptor, and the emission spectra of pQDs, serving as the donor. Additionally, to assess signal loss and colloidal stability, the optical characteristics of pCdTe QDs were analyzed over time. The fluorescence intensity and, as a result, the colloidal stability of the pCdTe QDs remained nearly unchanged for 20 days, facilitating their long-term application planning. In summary, the comprehensive characterization of the QDs-based biosensor involved the use of state-of-the-art equipment that provided insights into the size, shape, optical, structural, and morphological characteristics of the QDs. This multifaceted approach ensured a thorough understanding of the biosensor's performance, which is essential for its optimization and potential deployment in biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of thiolated DNA to QDs using ligand exchange process
ce:section.ce:para: Primer as a thiolated DNA oligonucleotide was directly attached to CdTe QDs via a ligand-exchange method. The ligand exchange process is a chemical technique that involves substituting capping ligands on QDs with desired ligands that have strong coordination capabilities and also exhibit high hydrophilicity [29]. Primer based modified quantum dots (pQDs) represent a significant category of fluorescent nanomaterials due to their diverse applications, which include the assembly of nanosensors. Besides, the primary reason for choosing a primer is to ensure that it has a strong affinity for the core surface [30,31]

The optimization of the sensor's efficiency through the specific binding of the primer to CdTe QDs using a ligand exchange strategy is a crucial step in the process. Meanwhile, changing the ratio between the thiolated primers and QDs had the most significant effect on the relative levels of conjugation with an optimal ratio of 1:1 ( Fig. 3A). The optimization study revealed the stability of modified QDs within the pH range of 7–8 and the significant influence of temperature on the thiolated primer/QDs fluorescence performance. The ideal response temperature was found to be 25 °C (Fig. 3 B, and C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of nanobiosensor for detecting complementary target DNA (ctDNA)
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor is prepared by introducing and conjugating of thiolated primes, as capture DNA, with quantum dots (QDs). Cy3-labeled DNA primer was used as a quencher. Hybridization experiments were performed in RNase-free water and Tris buffer (pH 7.4) to identify ctDNA based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism ( Fig. 4A and B).

The fluorescence intensity of the nanobiosensor at 545 nm decreased with rising ctDNA concentration in both RNase-free water and Tris buffer (pH 7.4), with a more significant decrement in both media. This phenomenon provided the formation of a hybrid (sandwiched) system consisting of quencher DNA, QDs-DNA conjugate, and ctDNA ( Fig. 5). The hybridization of Cy3-labeled quencher DNA primer and QDs-DNA to the ctDNA brought the Cy3 fluorophore (acceptor) in close proximity to the CdTe QDs (donor). When CdTe QDs were excited at 325 nm, energy was efficiently transferred to the Cy3 fluorophore via the FRET system, resulting in QDs fluorescence quenching. This quenching signal can be used for the detection and quantification of the complementary target DNA (ctDNA), which represents the SARS-CoV-2 virus.

To examine the specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the entitled nano biosensor, the distinct performance characteristics of the system in differentiating the target complementary DNA molecule, which is a specific segment of RNA of a virus, from both three-base mismatched and non-target molecules (non-complementary DNA) were effectively assessed and have been depicted in Figs. 4C and 5. This section has provided a clearer and more effective comparison between a sandwiched hybrid system (comprising pQDs nanoprobe, quencher DNA, and complementary DNA) and a non-sandwiched system (which includes pQDs nanoprobe, quencher DNA, and non-complementary DNA with three-base mismatches). Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of the QDs-DNA nanoprobe showed a slight increase in the presence of non-complementary DNA and three-base mismatches compared to the complementary DNA. This indicated that the pQDs nanoprobe could differentiate between non-complementary DNA and complementary DNA. Moreover, the resulting of nano biosensor was in good reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations of the detection of designed nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The limitation of detection (LOD) was persevering the analysis of the effect of complementary target DNA (ctDNA) at different concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of the pQDs nanobiosensor. The Stern-Volmer relationship (1) was used to determine the LOD of the ctDNA measurement [25].
(1’) F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q]

In this equation, the fluorescence intensity of pQDs biosensor in the absence and nearness of ctDNA have been indicated as F0 and F, individually. Ksv is the quenching constant parameter of the sandwich system, and [Q] is the ctDNA concentration.

The Stern-Volmer relationship could be considered as a numerical equation that portrayed the extinguishing of fluorescence within the nearness of a quencher molecule. The equation relates the fluorescence intensity of a fluorophore to the concentration of a quencher.

In this regard, the Stern-Volmer equation was Y = 25.651x + 1 with a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9956 ( Fig. 6A). Furthermore, determining the limit of detection (LOD) is a vital step in evaluating the performance of biosensors. The Stern-Volmer relationship can be a useful tool for establishing the LOD in these devices. In the current study, the LOD for ctDNA detection was assessed based on Stern-Volmer equation, and the LOD was calculated to be 1.71 × 10−9 mol/L.

The SARS-CoV-2 RNA was extracted from patient saliva and was added to the biosensor mixture instead of the complementary or non-complementary sequences. The mixture was then analyzed using fluorescence to approve the presence of SARS-CoV-2. The study's results were compared with the widely-used RT-PCR method detecting viral RNA by amplifying it through enzymatic reactions. In other words, the thiolated primer/ quantum dots combination’s capacity was examined to identify SARS-CoV-2 in the fifty human throat swab samples. Virous amounts of SARS-CoV-2 were added to nanobiosensor in RNass free water, and the levels of SARS-CoV-2 in human throat swab samples were measured in order to confirm the performance of pQDs nanobiosensor. These analyses indicated that the RSD values ranged from 3.50 % to 4.50 %. Besides, the analytical data from fluorescence technique in comparison with real-time PCR have been shown in Table 3. Statistical significance was determined using Chi-square and Fisher’s exact t-test. The parameters for t-test were obtained with P < 0.05 indicating no significance difference between two groups. In this research, real-time RT-PCR was utilized as the reference test, and the sensitivity, and specificity of FL were determined based on the results from the PCR. The sensitivity of FL was determined by dividing the count of samples that were positive for SARS-CoV-2 by both FL and RT-PCR by the total number of samples that were positive by RT-PCR. The specificity of FL was ascertained by dividing the count of samples that were negative for both FL and RT-PCR by the total number of samples that were negative by RT-PCR. Positive predictive value (PPV) of FL was calculated by dividing the count of samples that tested positive for both FL and RT-PCR by the total number of samples that were positive by FL. Negative predictive value (NPV) was calculated by dividing the number of samples negative by both FL and RT-PCR by the total number of FL negative samples. The overall sensitivity of FL for SARS-CoV-2 detection was 68 %, and the specificity was 80 %. Furthermore, PPV was 65 % and NVP was 80 %. All in all, this nanobiosensor illustrated efficient accuracy around 15 minutes. This rapid detection capability is pivotal in swiftly identifying and isolating infected individuals, thereby preventing further transmission. The speed and accuracy demonstrated by the nanobiosensor in this study underscored its potential as a rapid and effective tool for the early detection of SARS-CoV-2 infection [20].

To evaluate the effectiveness of the nanosensor for analyzing real samples, the system was tested to distinguish between positive samples (V1 and V2, which contain RNA from the SARS-CoV-2 virus) and negative samples (V3 which do not contain SARS-CoV-2 RNA). As shown in Fig. 6B, the hybrid system successfully differentiated between the positive samples (V1 and V2) and the negative ones (V3) across various concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of the positive samples, which included a hybrid system consisting of QDs-DNA nanoprobe, quencher DNA, and the SARS-CoV-2 RNA target, decreased significantly compared to the original system that only contained the QDs-DNA nanoprobe and quencher DNA in RNase-free water. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of the negative samples, which lacked SARS-CoV-2 RNA, and contained only the QDs-DNA nanoprobe and quencher DNA, showed a slight increase when compared to the control sample in RNase-free water.

Regarding SARS-CoV-2 infection, including its transmission, symptoms, and the universal impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, early detection and global efforts to control the virus's spread is necessary. This study presented a straightforward and quick method for detecting the SARS-CoV-2 virus using the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. The refined modified thiolated primer-based quantum dots exhibited strong fluorescence intensity in optimal conditions. The detailed characterization of the QDs-based nanobiosensor utilized advanced equipment to analyze the size, shape, optical, structural, and morphological properties of the quantum dots. This comprehensive approach provided a deep understanding of the biosensor's performance, which is crucial for its optimization and potential use in biomedical applications. The nanobiosensor (QDs-DNA), in conjunction with a Cy3 dye-labeled primer quencher, allowed for the quantitative detection of complementary target DNA (ctDNA). This efficient detection relied on the formation of a sandwiched (hybridized) structure that led to fluorescence quenching of QDs-DNA. In this structure, the quantum dots acted as the donor, positioned near the Cy3 dye, serving as the acceptor, thereby activating the FRET mechanism. The strong linear correlation observed between the F0/F value and ctDNA concentration indicated a wide linear range and a low detection limit of 1.71 × 10−9 mol/L for ctDNA detection. The performance of nanobiosensor for fifty real patient samples showed high compatibility with real-time PCR results, indicating the biosensor's accuracy and effectiveness in detecting SARS-CoV-2. In summary, this study highlighted innovative use of quantum dot-based FRET technology combined with functionalized primers for ultra-sensitive and specific detection of SARS-CoV-2, addressing the need for rapid, reliable, and accessible viral diagnostics beyond traditional PCR.

Masoumeh Khanahmadi: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Mahdieh Sharifi: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ghasem Rezanejade Bardajee: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conﬂicts of interest in this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this project from the SBU.
